title: Portland City Council Climate, Resilience and Land Use Committee 03/12/26

chapters.summary: Last meeting of the Climate Resilience and Land Use Committee to order. It is Thursday, March 12th at 9:32 AM. Councilor Avalos is here. Ashley Murillo will read the statement of conduct.
chapters.headline: Councilor Avalos chairs Climate Resilience and Land Use Committee
chapters.gist: Council Climate Resilience and Land Use Committee

chapters.summary: To testify before this committee in person or virtually, you must sign up in advance. Registration for virtual testimony closes 1 hour prior to the meeting. Your microphone will be muted when your time is over. Anyone who fails to leave once ejected is subject to arrest for trespass.
chapters.headline: To testify before this committee in person or virtually, you must sign up
chapters.gist: Committee Meeting

chapters.summary: This morning we have 3 items on our agenda. We're going to be considering an appointment to the Historic Landmarks Commission. Then we will go over a resolution to expedite plans for upzoning the Inner East Side for All. And finally, we'll hear an update from our Chief Sustainability Officer and climate staff on the city and county's climate and energy trends.
chapters.headline: This morning we have 3 items on our agenda: reappointment to Historic Commission
chapters.gist: Portland City Council: Appointments to the Historic Landmarks Commission

chapters.summary: The Historic Landmarks Commission is seeking the reappointment of a current Commissioner for a second 4-year term. The commission provides leadership and expertise on maintaining and enhancing Portland's historic and architectural heritage. They will present their State of the City Preservation Report at their hearing on March 30th at 1:30.
chapters.headline: Council is seeking reappointment of Portland's Historic Landmarks Commission
chapters.gist: Portland Historic Landmarks Commission Reappointment

chapters.summary: The Historic Landmarks Commission provides expertise on maintaining and enhancing Portland's historic and architectural heritage. Peggy Knoll has been serving on the commission since 2022. If reappointed, her term will run from August 3rd of this year until August 2nd of 2030. With this reappointment, all of the commission's 7 positions will be filled.
chapters.headline: Portland City Council is considering a reappointment to the Historic Landmarks Commission
chapters.gist: Reappointment to the Historic Landmarks Commission

chapters.summary: Reappointments do not go through a new recruitment. There is the option, uh, it's— it depends on the current member. Is there a reason that it's early? Well, we were told that there is a period of no appointments coming to Council.
chapters.headline: I just wanted to quickly ask, um, two questions regarding reappointments
chapters.gist: Reappointments to Peggy Knoll

chapters.summary: My question isn't really about this reappointment, but sort of about the process in general. We do have a diverse membership. With this broadened membership, they have been continuing to focus on more cultural resources than physical architectural. Can I assume that we will hear that date at— in the next week or so, do you think?
chapters.headline: Councilor Murillo asks question about reappointment of Historic Landmarks Commission
chapters.gist: Appointment of a Historic Landmarks Commission Member

chapters.summary: Councilor Mitch Green: This resolution directs the city administrator to come back to council within 90 days with a concrete plan to accelerate Inner Eastside rezoning. The goal is to have council vote on zoning changes by June 30th, 2027. Green: It's a perfect example of using our power as a legislative body to clearly state our priorities.
chapters.headline: Measure directs city administrator to develop accelerated timeline for Inner Eastside Air area planning
chapters.gist: Portland City Council passes Resolution to Accelerate Inner Rezoning

chapters.summary: Mitch Green: Growth is a function of land use. If we change our zoning maps to enable more density per unit of land, then we can spread the cost of that land over more units of housing. Green: What is the legally required minimum process for the City of Portland to move?
chapters.headline: Mitch Green: Portland is facing a significant housing shortage
chapters.gist: Portland City Council passes Resolution on zoning reform

chapters.summary: Heidi Hart is a volunteer with Portland Neighbors Welcome. She is also a registered volunteer lobbyist for the Inner Eastside for All campaign. Sorry for all the, uh, technical difficulties today. In my defense, I pleaded daylight savings time as an excuse.
chapters.headline: Heidi Hart: Sorry for all the technical difficulties today
chapters.gist: Portland Neighborhoods Committee Meeting

chapters.summary: Relegalize apartments and neighborhood corner stores in Portland's Eastside. Nearly two-thirds of the Inner East Side is still zoned low density today. More neighbors means more customers for local businesses. This is the single best lever the city has and we should use it.
chapters.headline: Relegalize apartments and neighborhood corner stores in Portland's Eastside
chapters.gist: Portland Neighbors Welcome: Relegalize apartments in the Inner East

chapters.summary: Council resolution asks for a 90-day report laying out a path to zoning changes by June 2027. 120,000 potential additional new homes mean more living options for all ages, wages, and stages of life. What we need now is a clear signal from this council that this is a high priority.
chapters.headline: Council resolution asks for 90-day report laying out path to zoning changes by 2027
chapters.gist: Proposition 4: Upzoning the Inner

chapters.summary: Curtis Reichstadt: I stand here before you with strong support for Build on Inner Eastside Initiative for All. He says FAR, floor area ratios and step-down heights are artificial constraints to creating more density. Reichstadt urges committee to approve this and also consider a 10 or 20-year ban on FAR for affordable housing.
chapters.headline: Curtis Reichstadt supports Build on Inner Eastside Initiative for All
chapters.gist: Developers speak out for Build on Inner

chapters.summary: A denser zoned inner east side will open up an abundance of opportunity for local builders. The status quo process for this project is potentially upwards of 3 years. Accelerating this common-sense project is the city meeting the moment.
chapters.headline: Jake Antles: Portland needs to accelerate housing project amid housing crisis
chapters.gist: Portland Housing Crisis: Accelerating the East Side Project

chapters.summary: Sam Diaz: Community fought long and hard for the anti-displacement protections in our comprehensive plan. He says let's remember our values in this conversation. Diaz: Portland is a national leader. National funders are looking at Oregon and funding Portland Neighbors Welcome.
chapters.headline: Sam Diaz: Portland fought long and hard for anti-displacement protections
chapters.gist: Portland Neighbors Welcomes Anti-

chapters.summary: Sarah Taylor: Lloyd Center is scheduled for demolition, and it's scheduled for 14 towers of housing. That is an indoor walking trail for elderly, for the disabled, for so many people. Taylor: Make part of your study be to look at how we can use that footprint of 29 acres for low-income housing.
chapters.headline: Three people have signed up to give public testimony on proposed Lloyd Center demolition
chapters.gist: Public testimony at the Portland City Council meeting

chapters.summary: Wes Ayers: Large portions of Portland's urban core remain zoned exclusively for single-family homes. He says Portland needs both substantial upzoning and zoning reform. The Inner East Side is the right place to act, he says.
chapters.headline: Wes Ayers supports Portland Neighbors Welcome resolution on zoning changes
chapters.gist: Portland Neighborhoods Welcomes Resolution on Upzoning

chapters.summary: Indy Namco: Verde supports resolution that would direct city staff to complete the zoning work for the Inner Eastside by June of 2027. She says the city has been artificially suppressing more homes in the high-resource and high-opportunity neighborhoods of the inner east side. Indy: This resolution can be our intervention by bringing more homes to the most vulnerable areas of Portland.
chapters.headline: Verde supports resolution directing city staff to complete zoning work for Inner Eastside
chapters.gist: Inner Eastside for All

chapters.summary: BPS supports this resolution. We're already underway with scoping and working on an engagement plan. The 90-day report back is reasonable from our perspective. Our goal is to investigate, to figure out what the constraints are, and then do our best to try to ameliorate them.
chapters.headline: Portland Neighbors Welcome supports 90-day timeline for land use study
chapters.gist: Portland Neighbors Welcome on the 90-Day Report

chapters.summary: Does every jurisdiction have a planning commission, or are there some places just like proposals come straight from staff to the city council? I think there does need to be an opportunity for local businesses residents, developers to have meaningful conversations. I would really advocate to keep that part of the timeline.
chapters.headline: Does every jurisdiction have a planning commission, or are there some places lacking
chapters.gist: Planning Commission: Does Every Local Municipality Have a Planning Commission

chapters.summary: Council finds that prior public engagement provides a substantial foundation for the project. Is that feasible, or is that like a, you know, a time bomb? The Bureau has done a lot of work to actively reach out to people. There is value in having public engagement on a more street-by-street level.
chapters.headline: Council finds prior public engagement provides a substantial foundation for the project
chapters.gist: Prior Public Engagement on the Portland Housing Plan

chapters.summary: Mayor: Creating 140,000 units of capacity in zoning isn't necessarily gonna cause those units to be built. In inner southeast, the zoning tends to be focused on the corridors. Mayor: We really need to be building stacked flats that are accessible in 2 or 3 bedrooms.
chapters.headline: There's capacity in this area for an additional 20,000 units
chapters.gist: Additional multifamily units needed in inner southeast Portland

chapters.summary: Councilor Ryan: What makes us think that changing this will get at least 20,000 more? Councilor Novick: The market feasibility question definitely came up in residential infill project. Councilor Murillo: There's still a lag time with statewide solutions. The work begins.
chapters.headline: Ryan Murillo: Changing zoning would allow for lower rents for some projects
chapters.gist: Council: Zoning Maps Need to Be Changed

chapters.summary: Ting Lu: There's a desire to have a more clearly defined time frame. Will this analysis also include the impact of or influence to the conversation on SDCs? How do we improve transit service in this area? Councilor Kunal: I'm very supportive of Inner East Side for All.
chapters.headline: Councilor Kunal wants a defined timeframe for Inner East Side project
chapters.gist: Portland Neighbors on the 90-Day Project

chapters.summary: Councilor Knoll suggested an amendment to the resolution. It would identify how the city can shorten the overall timeline to council's consideration of zoning changes to no later than June 30th, 2027. With 3 ayes, 1 nay, and 1 absent, the amendment is approved.
chapters.headline: Councilor Knoll supports moving resolution to full council with recommendation
chapters.gist: Council Resolution number 2026-106 to be adopted

chapters.summary: Councilor Novick requested item 3, Multnomah County climate and energy trends. Vivian Satterfield and Kyle Diesner will provide an update for the committee on Portland's progress to date on carbon emission reductions. If you can rip through the whole thing quickly, I think it'd be really beneficial.
chapters.headline: Councilor Novick requests item 3 on Multnomah County climate and energy trends
chapters.gist: Council Committee on Multnomah County Climate and Energy Trends

chapters.summary: Vivian Satterfield: This is the last meeting and the last agenda item of the Climate Resilience and Land Use Committee. She says the Sustainability and Climate Commission will advise the mayor, city administrator, and city council on issues related to sustainability and climate. And so with that, she'll hand it over to Kyle Deesner to answer your questions.
chapters.headline: Climate Resilience and Land Use Committee meets to discuss carbon emissions
chapters.gist: Committee on Climate Resilience and Land Use

chapters.summary: Kyle Deesner is a climate policy analyst at the Bureau of Planning and Sustainability. He has been responsible for our emission inventory since 2010. The data is available on our public-facing dashboard as a resource for everyone.
chapters.headline: Kyle Deesner: I'm responsible for our emission inventory since 2010
chapters.gist: During the Committee Meeting on Climate and Energy

chapters.summary: Multnomah County carbon emissions peaked in the year 2000 and have come down since that time. Emissions have declined 26% since 1990, while population has grown 38% and jobs have increased 35%. We really need to bend this curve down much quicker to reach our targets.
chapters.headline: Multnomah County carbon emissions have declined 26% since 1990
chapters.gist: Portland County Carbon Emissions

chapters.summary: Today our electricity use is lower than it was in 1990 for our base year. The largest source of our electricity generation currently is natural gas. We expect that number to spike back up as the utilities start to comply with the state renewable requirements by 2030.
chapters.headline: The largest source of our electricity generation currently is natural gas
chapters.gist: California's electricity and gas consumption trends

chapters.summary: Transportation fuel pricing is the single largest policy driver. Diesel sales are up 70% compared to gasoline, which is down 20% over the same period. We are working on a new transportation inventory method that will give us greater insight into the transportation sector.
chapters.headline: The bulk of our transportation emissions come from gasoline and diesel
chapters.gist: Transportation emissions, Portland

chapters.summary: consumption-based emissions are life cycle emissions that are produced as a result of consumer spending here in Multnomah County. These emissions are almost double the emissions we produce locally. There is an international push to move towards more adoption of targets in this sector.
chapters.headline: Multnomah County has consumption-based emission inventory compared to sector-based
chapters.gist: Multnomah County's Consumption-based Emissions

chapters.summary: The consumption-based inventory does allow us to see emissions by lifecycle phase. 66% of those emissions are coming from the production of things. All of this data is on a climate and energy dashboard that's online and available to the public.
chapters.headline: 66% of Oregon's consumption-based emissions come from production
chapters.gist: Portland's emissions data, including consumption-based data

chapters.summary: Pacificorp customers are much more carbon intensive now, significantly, than PGE. Both utilities are required to hit the state's renewable energy targets, which start to go into effect in 2030. It is a good idea to be strategic in how we deploy those things.
chapters.headline: Pacificorp customers are significantly more carbon intensive than PGE customers
chapters.gist: Final Report of the Climate and Land Use Committee

chapters.summary: Councilor: The conversations around climate are not over just because this committee is going away. Land use decisions, which are often tied to climate, will fall under the Housing Committee. With that, we will end the Climate Committee, the final one, at 11:29 today.
chapters.headline: The Climate Committee will end its work at 11:29 today
chapters.gist: A Final Statement from the Climate Committee
